solution.p: We will factor 16y^{2}+8y+1 where a=16, b=8, and c=1.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial 16y^{2}+8y+1 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]16y^{2}+8y+1\amp =\left(4y\right)^2+2\left(4y\right)\left(1\right)+\left(1\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(4y+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(4y+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(4y+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (16) \cdot (1) = 16.

The number 16 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)
ewline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 8, and this sum happens with 4+4=8 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]16y^{2}+8y+1\amp =16y^{2}+4y+4y+1
ewline \amp =4y\mathopen{}\left(4y+1ight)+4y+1
ewline \amp =\left(4y+1ight)\mathopen{}\left(4y+1ight)
ewline \amp =\left(4y+1ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 16y^{2}+8y+1 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:

The first term's coefficient, 16, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(16)\amp 2(8)\amp 4(4)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp -1(-1)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 16y^{2}+8y+1 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]16y^{2}+8y+1\amp =\left(4y+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(4y+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(4y+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
